These fragments do not repeat the same implementation or logic. The two fragments do not duplicate the same implementation in a way that would violate DRY. `reject_symlinks_from_root` performs a distinct root-to-leaf ancestor scan for a different trust boundary, while `reject_symlinks_below` is a thin adapter around `infra_reject_symlinks_below` that only normalizes error types. The overlap is limited to error classification, which is intentional boundary code rather than repeated business logic.

violation.refactor_proposal: Refactor `reject_symlinks_from_root` to compute the absolute path, derive its filesystem root component as the trusted root, and delegate to `crate::track::symlink_guard::reject_symlinks_below`. Keep only the `SymlinkGuardError` adapter locally so symlink and I/O errors are mapped consistently with the existing below-root wrapper.

records.rationale: The changed fragment reimplements the same symlink-component traversal and `symlink_metadata` rejection logic that `track::symlink_guard` is explicitly meant to centralize. The behavioral surface differs only in the chosen trust boundary: `reject_symlinks_from_root` absolutizes the input and checks from the filesystem root, while the shared helper checks below a supplied trusted root. That difference can be represented by deriving the filesystem root/trusted boundary and delegating, rather than duplicating the traversal, NotFound handling, and symlink/error classification logic.

violation.refactor_proposal: Refactor `reject_symlinks_from_root` to absolutize the path, derive the filesystem root/trusted boundary from the absolute path, call `crate::track::symlink_guard::reject_symlinks_below(&absolute_path, root)`, ignore the returned existence bool, and map `InvalidInput` to `SymlinkGuardError::SymlinkFound` while mapping other errors to `SymlinkGuardError::Io`. If root-inclusive behavior is truly required, extend the shared helper with a small boundary option rather than keeping a second traversal implementation.

records.rationale: The changed fragment reimplements the same symlink-guard algorithm already present in `track::symlink_guard::reject_symlinks_below`: collect ancestors, reverse root-to-leaf, call `symlink_metadata`, reject symlink file types, ignore `NotFound`, and propagate other I/O errors. The main differences are boundary selection (filesystem root instead of a trusted root) and error adaptation, which are not enough to justify duplicating the traversal/stat logic. The adjacent documentation even identifies the shared helper as the consolidation point.

records.rationale: The changed fragment reimplements the core symlink-guard algorithm already centralized in `track::symlink_guard::reject_symlinks_below`: collect ancestors, reverse to root-to-leaf order, call `symlink_metadata`, reject symlink file types, ignore `NotFound`, and propagate other I/O errors. The main difference is only the boundary selection: full filesystem root versus a supplied trusted root. That boundary difference can be expressed by deriving the root/trusted boundary and delegating to the shared helper, while keeping local error mapping in the adapter.

The fragments share some low-level symlink-stat traversal structure, but they are not duplicating a single reusable implementation in a way that creates a DRY defect. The changed code has different semantics: it first absolutizes relative paths from the current directory, walks all ancestors from root, and maps errors into a domain-specific `SymlinkGuardError`, while the existing helper is parameterized by `trusted_root`, stops at that boundary, and returns `Result<bool, std::io::Error>`. That is a thin adapter around related filesystem checks, not an unjustified copy of the same business logic.
